answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation says extending the free trial from 14 to 30 days will lift paid conversion, but briefing fact #2 reports the A/B test found the 30-day trial converted LOWER than 14 days.
answer_key.note: Distractors: signup-volume data (#1) and a competitor-benchmark anecdote (#3) make the longer trial sound supported. Buried contradiction: the company's OWN A/B test in #2 showed 30-day trials converted at 6.1% vs 8.4% for 14-day — the longer trial reduced conversion, directly opposite the rec's claim.

recommendation: Extend the free trial from 14 to 30 days globally. A longer runway lets prospects reach their 'aha' moment, reduces signup-abandonment pressure, and aligns us with category leaders. We expect this to materially increase paid conversion and ARR, and because our prior testing supports a longer-trial uplift it can ship as a default change without a further gated rollout.

briefing.context: Growth team is deciding whether to extend the standard free trial length to improve paid conversion. Decision: ship 30-day trial globally as the new default.

facts.text: #1 Monthly trial signups average 12,000; signup volume has been flat for three quarters and is not the constraint on revenue.

#2 A 50/50 A/B test (n=18,400, run last quarter) measured paid conversion: the 14-day trial converted at 8.4% and the 30-day trial converted at 6.1% — the longer trial converted LOWER, with significance p<0.01.
#3 Two category-leading competitors advertise 30-day trials in their marketing pages.

prompts.B: Review this growth recommendation and note whether it is sound.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING review. The Generator defends the recommendation; the Challenger attacks it, but a concern counts ONLY if it cites a checkable artifact by fact-id (#n). Iterate to convergence on a corrected recommendation.
prompts.T: You are a tough skeptic. Push back hard on this recommendation and find what's wrong with it.

outputs.B: Reasonable proposal. Longer trials are a common growth lever, competitor alignment is sensible, and the reasoning about reaching the activation moment is plausible. Seems fine to proceed.

S.generator: A 30-day trial gives prospects more runway to activate, matches category leaders per #3, and signup volume is healthy per #1, so we should capture more conversions.
S.challenger: The core claim is refuted by our own data in #2: the rec says the longer trial will 'materially increase paid conversion' and that 'prior testing supports a longer-trial uplift,' but #2's A/B test (n=18,400, p<0.01) found 30-day converted at 6.1% vs 8.4% for 14-day — the longer trial converted LOWER. The recommendation contradicts its own briefing.
S.converged: Do NOT extend to 30 days globally; #2 shows it reduces conversion. Keep the 14-day default. If pursuing activation, test it via a gated experiment on the activation moment itself rather than trial length, since #1 confirms signup volume is not the constraint.

outputs.T: I doubt this moves the needle. Longer trials usually just let people procrastinate. Where's the proof this works? This smells like a vanity change and I wouldn't trust the ARR projection at all.

concerns.text: The recommendation predicts higher conversion from a 30-day trial, but #2's A/B test shows 30-day converted LOWER (6.1% vs 8.4%, p<0.01).
atomic: The 30-day trial converted at 6.1% vs 8.4% for 14-day per #2
The recommendation's predicted uplift contradicts that result

concerns.text: The rec implies signup growth is part of the value, but #1 says signup volume is flat and not the revenue constraint, so trial length is the wrong lever.
atomic: Signup volume is flat and not the constraint per #1

concerns.text: Competitor alignment is a fair supporting point for the longer trial.
atomic: Two category leaders advertise 30-day trials per #3

concerns.text: The recommendation says prior testing supports a longer-trial uplift, but the briefing's own A/B test shows the opposite: 30-day converted at 6.1% versus 14-day at 8.4%, significant at p<0.01. This directly contradicts the core premise.
atomic: The A/B test showed 30-day converted lower (6.1%) than 14-day (8.4%)
The recommendation's premise contradicts the cited test

concerns.text: Shipping 'as a default change without a further gated rollout' is exactly backwards given a significant negative result — you'd be globally deploying a change your own data predicts will reduce conversion.
atomic: Skipping a gated rollout deploys a change predicted to lower conversion globally

concerns.text: The ARR-increase claim has no mechanism behind it — signup volume is flat and explicitly not the revenue constraint, so 'reduces signup-abandonment pressure' targets a problem that isn't limiting revenue.
atomic: Signup volume is flat and not the revenue constraint

concerns.text: 'Aligns us with category leaders' is cargo-cult reasoning — copying two competitors' marketed trial length tells you nothing about whether it converts, and your own experiment already outweighs their marketing pages.
atomic: Matching competitors' advertised trial length is not evidence it converts better

concerns.text: In my experience longer trials erode urgency and let prospects forget the product, which is a plausible explanation for the measured drop.
atomic: Longer trials can erode purchase urgency
